author: @Gwydion67
text:
Unbelievable! 😲 A generator giving more than a couple of mA. ⚡ I firstly thought, I misheard. 👂

Hm, I only have one old battery drill, actually in the pipeline of being converted from NiCd to Li-Ion cells.
Hm, rethinking...🤔💭
- I *do* have two modern Li-Ion drills, plus a hammer drill for the mains.
- I *do* have a 7,2kWh LiFePO4 battery, never getting full by my tiny off-grid PV.
- I'm sitting in front of a laptop computer all day long, called "home office", missing my daily bicycle commuting to work badly, plus consuming that laptop's amount of extra energy.
🤔❔...1 + 1 = ...2❕✅ ☝😃

author: @nicholascup126
text:
Howdy,
A couple of ideas for a better view of your  meter.
In photography we would place a black reflector up so it would be reflected off of a surface (like your meter) which would give it much better visibility. Black sweaters work great in a pinch.
And if it is because the backlight is too bright we would place a neutral density filter over the screen to bring the intensity down if there was no way to adjust it. 
I hope this helps, cause I love your channel.

author: @NorthernKitty
text:
I've been using those voltage regulators to make 3D-printed lighting fixtures using 12V LED strips.  That way I can power the LEDs with 3.7V lithium battery sources (like a cell phone battery).  I've also used them to step-down voltages from 12V batteries to power 5V electronics, such as running a Raspberry Pi or a Bluetooth sound bar off a 12V battery.

I agree, it's mind-blowing how cheap they are!!  Along with a host of other small electronic circuits, such as charging circuits, speed controllers, thermostat relays, etc.  I truly wish they had "Dollar Store" off-the-shelf electronics like this when I was a teen, I would have had a blast!!  (Well, I'm having a blast with them now, but wish I didn't have to wait 40 years, LOL!! 🤣)   It definitely beats out the HeathKit and Radio Shack parts I grew up with!

author: @dgpreston5593
text: Hey Rob, really cool.  Adding a four diode bridge to rectify the forward/reverse would allow you to mount a really long crank arm inside a spring from a shock absorber with a wind-levered upright.  Presto! A bladeless wind-powered generator. The spring keeps the lever arm upright, and the wind leans the long lever arm to and fro.    Nice mention of the $1 voltage regulator.

author: @scotttovey
text:
If you don't want to go through the bother of making a case for your generator use the drill driver case the motor comes in.

Keep the motor in the drill driver case.
Take a bad battery pack that goes to that particular drill driver and empty the cells out of it taking care to note the contacts that mate with the internal contacts of the drill driver.
Unsolder those contacts from the battery cells and solder wire on to it and insert the contacts to the base where they belong.
Note, you may have to fashion a couple of dummy cells out of wood dowels to insure the contacts stay in place. Rather than gluing them in place; keep them removable for future maintenance.
Solder wires to your voltage regulator and mount it inside the battery case.
If you regulator has switches or a pot; cut access holes to them or it in the base of the battery pack case.
Insert the battery case into the drill driver as normal, then attach a handle and crank away.

The format of the drill driver also means that you can mount it on a board or other structure without too much trouble.

author: @ezequieladrianminniti6252
text: be aware even lm2596 cheap circiuts do have short circiut protection, do not have reverse voltage protection, so turning handle backwards will fry the circiut. protect it using normal forward current diode or schottky diodes for lower voltage drop.

Maybe I should start making a habit of throwing in a schottky in my designs.
